primary_diagnosis.name: Mast cell activation syndrome, cutaneous-predominant acute flare (severe acute urticaria with dermatographism), NOT anaphylaxis

pathophysiology_summary: In a patient with an established mast-cell activation disorder, a physical trigger (heat) provokes cutaneous mast-cell degranulation, releasing histamine and other mediators into the skin. This produces widespread urticaria, intense pruritus, flushing and dermatographism (mechanical stimulation triggers localised wheals). Crucially, the degranulation here is confined to the skin: there is no laryngeal oedema, no bronchospasm, no gastrointestinal involvement and no distributive hypotension, so ASCIA anaphylaxis criteria are NOT met — anaphylaxis requires acute skin/mucosal change PLUS respiratory and/or persistent severe GI and/or cardiovascular (hypotension) involvement, OR isolated hypotension after a known allergen. The tachycardia (HR ~105) is explained by anxiety, pruritus and histamine-driven vasodilation, not by shock. The management target is histamine-receptor blockade (H1 up-titration plus an H2 antagonist) and trigger removal (cooling), with a low threshold to escalate if any airway or circulatory feature appears, because an explosive cutaneous mast-cell flare can, uncommonly, be the leading edge of an evolving systemic reaction.

differential.evidence_basis: Explosive widespread urticaria + flushing + tachycardia + patient fear of airway closure must trigger an immediate multi-system red-flag screen before anaphylaxis can be excluded.
Widespread pruritic wheals with dermatographism after a heat trigger in a patient with a known mast-cell condition.
Widespread rash, but the extreme rapidity of onset, intense pruritus, wheal morphology and dermatographism argue against it and there is no fever.
Cellulitis is unilateral, fixed, warm and febrile — incompatible with widespread, bilateral, shifting wheals and no fever.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, anaphylaxis MUST be held as the important-not-to-miss item and actively screened; it is not yet excludable.

differential.diagnosis: Cutaneous-predominant mast-cell activation flare (severe acute urticaria)
differential.evidence_basis: Once the patient reports no throat tightness, no dyspnoea/wheeze, no faintness and no GI symptoms, and remains normotensive-by-history, ASCIA criteria for anaphylaxis are not met and the reaction is confined to skin.

No respiratory, no cardiovascular and no GI involvement on a deliberate red-flag screen — single-organ (cutaneous) mast-cell activation does not satisfy anaphylaxis criteria. Remains a safety-net contingency, not the active diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The pivot of the case: excluding anaphylaxis on the patient-reportable NEGATIVE red-flag screen, and NOT over-escalating a purely cutaneous flare to ED/000.

differential.diagnosis: Mast cell activation syndrome — cutaneous-predominant acute flare, heat-triggered, with dermatographism
differential.evidence_basis: Known mast-cell disorder + heat trigger + widespread pruritic urticaria + dermatographism + OTC antihistamine failure, with a fully negative acute systemic red-flag screen. Managed as outpatient with antihistamine escalation and cooling, plus mandatory anaphylaxis safety-netting.

red_flags.description: Laryngeal / oropharyngeal oedema — throat tightness, difficulty swallowing, voice change (would signal airway involvement)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The single most important discriminator between a cutaneous flare and anaphylaxis. Its ABSENCE (patient reports normal throat, swallowing and voice) is what excludes airway-threatening anaphylaxis at this moment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If genuinely present but not asked about, an evolving airway anaphylaxis could be under-triaged and progress to occlusion.

red_flags.description: Lower-airway involvement — shortness of breath, wheeze, inability to speak in full sentences
red_flags.clinical_significance: Respiratory involvement is one limb of the ASCIA anaphylaxis criteria; its absence (speaks full sentences, no wheeze) supports a skin-limited flare.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed bronchospasm could delay recognition of systemic anaphylaxis.

red_flags.description: Circulatory compromise — dizziness/faintness on standing, presyncope, sense of impending collapse (hypotension proxy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distributive (anaphylactic) shock manifests as lightheadedness/faintness; its absence (up and pacing, not faint) argues strongly against anaphylaxis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Unrecognised hypotension is a life threat; must be screened before excluding anaphylaxis.

red_flags.description: Persistent severe gastrointestinal symptoms — repeated vomiting, severe abdominal cramps (an anaphylaxis limb after allergen exposure)
red_flags.clinical_significance: GI involvement can be a systemic-reaction limb; its absence further supports a skin-confined event.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed severe GI involvement could under-call a systemic reaction.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept by the vivid whole-body urticaria, flushing and tachycardia — and the source's 'rule out anaphylaxis' framing — into treating this as anaphylaxis, instructing an adrenaline autoinjector and sending to ED/000 despite a fully negative airway/circulatory/GI screen. This is the primary scored error: over-treating a purely cutaneous flare.
Reading HR ~105 as evidence of distributive shock, when it is explained by anxiety, itch and histamine vasodilation in a normotensive patient who is up and pacing.

The opposite failure: reassuring the patient it is 'just hives' and closing the encounter WITHOUT the mandatory anaphylaxis safety-net, so that if the flare does evolve to airway or circulatory involvement the patient does not know to call 000.
Simply advising another dose of the same standard OTC antihistamine that already failed, instead of up-titrating the non-sedating H1 antihistamine and adding an H2 antagonist per urticaria guidance.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal oropharyngeal airway examination (uvula position, posterior pharyngeal oedema)
Chest auscultation for wheeze / air entry
Measured blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature and SpO2
Clinician-elicited dermatographism on a controlled skin area
Serum tryptase or other mast-cell mediator assay
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a cutaneous-predominant mast-cell flare on the patient-reportable negative systemic screen; advise oral non-sedating H1 antihistamine up-titration plus an H2 antagonist and active cooling; arrange GP review within ~3 days to optimise the H1/H2 regimen; and give PROMINENT, specific anaphylaxis safety-netting to call 000 immediately if throat tightness, voice change, difficulty swallowing, wheeze/breathlessness, or faintness develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The dominant risk is OVER-action: labelling this anaphylaxis and escalating to adrenaline/000 when criteria are not met. The secondary risk is UNDER-action: closing without the anaphylaxis safety-net. Both are guarded against by (a) an explicit multi-system red-flag screen and (b) mandatory escalation edges.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASCIA Acute Management of Anaphylaxis Guidelines 2023
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Anaphylaxis requires acute onset skin/mucosal change PLUS respiratory and/or cardiovascular and/or persistent severe GI involvement (or isolated hypotension after a known allergen). Isolated urticaria without systemic involvement is not anaphylaxis and does not require adrenaline.

First-line treatment of acute/chronic urticaria is a second-generation non-sedating H1 antihistamine, up-titrated to up to fourfold the standard dose if the standard dose is inadequate; adding an H2 antagonist is a recognised adjunct.
